summary: Gruber corrects an error from a previous post about H.264 patent liability, acknowledging that individual licensees (not just MPEG-LA) can be sued for patent infringement. Despite this correction, he reinforces his core argument that H.264 is a safer bet than Ogg Theora when it comes to patent risk. He argues that Ogg Theora faces two categories of patent threat — submarine patents and MPEG-LA's own patent pool — while lacking any defensive patent arsenal. He concludes that if both formats are vulnerable, the rational choice is the technically superior one backed by an organization capable of mounting a legal defense.
key_points: Gruber corrects his earlier claim that MPEG-LA shields individual H.264 licensees from patent suits — under US patent law, even users of infringing inventions are liable
Ogg Theora faces a double patent threat: submarine patents (shared with H.264) plus potential claims from MPEG-LA's own H.264 patent pool
H.264's advantage is defensive: MPEG-LA can countersue using its patent pool, while Ogg Theora users are effectively unarmed in patent disputes
Being unpatented only means Ogg's developers won't sue users — it provides no protection against third-party patent claims
Gruber frames the piece as an indictment of the patent system itself, not of Ogg Theora
money_quotes: If the holder of a submarine patent decides to sue over Ogg Theora, Ogg Theora users are, effectively, unarmed.

That Ogg Theora is not patented only means that Ogg Theora's developers will not pursue Ogg Theora users for licensing fees or assert any restrictions. It does not mean that the holders of other patents will not claim Ogg Theora violates their patents.
If Ogg Theora takes off and becomes popular, would anyone be surprised if MPEG-LA started suing Ogg Theora users for licensing fees? I'd be surprised if they didn't.
The obvious choice (to me) would be the one that is both technically superior and is owned by a group that can ably defend itself in court, armed with patents of its own and experienced lawyers.
It's my intention here to indict our patent system, not Ogg Theora.
Only the deluded think that Ogg Theora — and software that uses it, such as Firefox — is any more immune from patent warfare than an unarmed man is immune from bullets.
key_insight: Being open-source and unpatented provides no defense against third-party patent claims — in fact, it leaves users more exposed than a proprietary standard backed by a well-armed patent pool.
